title: Links and navigation
assignment: Review the concepts from this section. If something is unclear, go back to the lesson.
groups.0.title: The link
groups.0.items.0.def: <a> is the tag, href says where. Between the tags is the word you click. The address is for the machine, the word is for the person — so the address can be ugly and hidden.
groups.0.items.1.def: Take the link text out of the sentence and read it alone. "Here"? Where? A blind person skims ONLY the links in a list — if they all say "here", they know where none of them lead. The word says the destination.
groups.0.items.2.def: An address left between the tags is read out character by character. It belongs in href, not on screen. On screen — a word.
groups.1.title: The paths
groups.1.items.0.def: Do not ask where the file is on the disk. Ask where it is relative to me. contact.html = next to me. pages/contact.html = in a subfolder. ../contact.html = one folder up.
groups.1.items.1.def: C:\Users\… works only on your machine. The test: send the folder to someone else and they open it — does it work? A drive letter or "Desktop" — no. And beware: on a server Bike.jpg and bike.jpg are different files.
groups.2.title: The menu
groups.2.items.0.def: A menu = <nav> (this is navigation) + <ul> (things of one kind) + one <li> per link. The dividers between them are CSS, not typed by hand. A blind person hears "list of three" and knows how big the menu is before entering it. A link mid-sentence is NOT nav — it is for the groups of links that move through the site.
